---
title: "Chikoo"
slug: chikoo
category: "Fruits, Veg & Botanicals"
category-slug: fruits-veg-botanicals
keywords: ["Chikoo", "Sapodilla", "Manilkara zapota", "Tropical Fruit", "Indian Fruit", "Milkshake", "Dessert", "Sweet Fruit"]
description: "Chikoo, botanically *Manilkara zapota* (sapodilla/sapota), originated in Mexico, Central America, and the Caribbean"
---

## History and Sourcing

[Chikoo](chikoo.qmd), botanically *Manilkara zapota* (sapodilla/sapota), originated in Mexico, Central America, and the Caribbean. Introduced to India by Portuguese traders, its name likely derives from 'chicle,' the tree's latex. It is now widely cultivated in Indian states like Maharashtra, Gujarat, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu, thriving in warm, humid conditions.

## Home Kitchen Use

In Indian homes, ripe chikoo is primarily consumed fresh or blended into milkshakes and smoothies due to its sweet, malty flavour and grainy texture. It is also incorporated into traditional desserts such as kheer and halwa, and occasionally used in jams, preserves, or fruit salads.

## Industrial Applications

Industrially, chikoo pulp is used in juices, nectars, and ice creams, providing natural sweetness and body. Its unique flavour and high sugar content also make it suitable for confectionery flavourings and dried fruit snacks. It contributes natural sugars and dietary fibre to certain health-oriented food products.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

Consumers often confuse the common Indian name '[Chikoo](chikoo.qmd)' with its botanical names, sapodilla or sapota. Another common challenge is identifying ripeness; an unripe chikoo is hard and astringent, while a ripe one is soft, sweet, and juicy. It should not be confused with other brown-skinned fruits like kiwi, which have a distinct tart flavour and different internal structure.
